content:
**The Unseen Cost of In-House AML Tools**

Early in my career, I was tasked with leading a team to develop an AI-based anti-money laundering (AML) tool in-house. We were convinced that building a custom solution would allow us to tailor it to our specific needs and avoid vendor lock-in.

As we delved deeper into the project, I struggled to balance the technical demands of building a robust AML engine with the need to ensure compliance with complex regulations. We spent countless hours fine-tuning our model, only to realize that we were neglecting the crucial aspect of ongoing monitoring and maintenance.

It wasn't until we faced a compliance audit that the full weight of our oversight became clear. The auditors pointed out that our custom solution lacked the transparency and documentation required to demonstrate our AML practices were effective. We had inadvertently created a compliance nightmare.

In hindsight, I realize that we underestimated the hidden cost of building in-house AML tools. It's not just about the upfront investment in technology, but also the ongoing resources required to maintain compliance. Today, I prioritize partnering with experienced vendors who can provide not only effective AML tools but also the necessary compliance expertise.

What lessons have you learned from your own experiences with in-house AML tool development?

inspired_by.topic: The hidden compliance cost of building AI-based AML tools in-house
inspired_by.concept: A GRC professional should care about this because it highlights the potential risks and compliance liabilities associated with building AI-based AML tools in-house.
